title: Scalable spin-nematic squeezing in multi-level dipole-interacting Rydberg atom arrays
abstract: We study the generation of metrologically useful entanglement in a three-level (spin-1) system naturally realized in arrays of dipole-interacting Rydberg atoms confined in optical tweezers. In the spin-quadrupolar operator basis, the interaction Hamiltonian decomposes into effective SU(2) subspaces, within which quench dynamics from product initial states generate scalable spin-nematic squeezing. For symmetric interactions, we identify a mapping to effective one-axis twisting within bright and dark manifolds and demonstrate that the squeezing parameter scales as $ξ^{2}\propto N^{-2/3}$ ($ξ^{2}\propto N^{-0.5}$) with system size for all-to-all (two-dimensional dipolar) couplings. In both cases the quantum Fisher information reaches $F_Q\propto N^{2}$. For antisymmetric interactions supplemented by a microwave drive we find a distinct two-axis countertwisting mechanism. This results in squeezing $ξ^{2}\propto N^{-0.7}$ for all-to-all interactions and moderate squeezing for dipolar interactions in 2D. Our results constitute a first theoretical step beyond the well-studied qubit setting toward scalable entanglement generation in qudit systems with dipolar interactions, directly relevant to current Rydberg tweezer experiments.
summary: This paper explores how to generate scalable entanglement in three-level Rydberg atom arrays using dipole-dipole interactions. It shows that symmetric interactions lead to one-axis twisting-like squeezing, while antisymmetric interactions can produce more efficient two-axis countertwisting. These findings provide a theoretical foundation for using multi-level atoms in advanced quantum sensing applications.
main_problem: Investigating the generation of scalable spin-nematic (quadrupolar) squeezing and entanglement in multi-level (qutrit) Rydberg atom arrays.
main_result: The study identifies that symmetric interactions enable scalable squeezing via One-Axis Twisting (OAT) dynamics, while antisymmetric interactions with a microwave drive enable Two-Axis Countertwisting (TAT) mechanisms.
method: The authors use Exact Diagonalization for all-to-all interaction models and the Discrete Truncated Wigner Approximation (dTWA) for 2D dipolar interaction simulations.
model_or_system: A three-level (spin-1) system realized in arrays of Rydberg atoms in optical tweezers, featuring dipole-dipole interactions and microwave driving.
key_observables: Squeezing parameter (xi^2) and Quantum Fisher Information (FQ).
important_parameters: Interaction ratio (Jr), interaction range/decay (alpha), and system size (N).
main_assumptions: The dTWA is used as a semiclassical approximation for large-scale 2D dynamics and is benchmarked against exact results.
figures_summary: Figure 1 provides a model overview of the Rydberg system; Figure 3 and 4 show squeezing and QFI scaling for the bright manifold under all-to-all interactions.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the three-level Rydberg model, analyzes the Hamiltonian decomposition into SU(2) subspaces for symmetric and antisymmetric regimes, presents scaling results for different interaction ranges, and validates the semiclassical approximation.
why_interesting: It extends well-known qubit-based squeezing protocols to qutrit systems and demonstrates how spatial interaction geometry (all-to-all vs. 2D dipolar) impacts the scalability of entanglement for quantum metrology.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Model overview. Rydberg states with alternat- ing parity define a three-level system labeled {|1⟩, |2⟩, |3⟩}. Microwaves Ωi drive transitions between adjacent levels, and dipolar interactions J1(2) ij induce pairwise spin exchange within the 1–2 and 2–3 sublevels between atoms at sites i and j with strength ∼r−3 ij .

FIG. 3. Entanglement dynamics for all-to-all interactions. Quantum Fisher information (left) and squeezing parame- ter (right) as a function of the interaction ratio Jr = J2/J1 for N = 49 (top) and system size N (bottom).

FIG. 4. Entanglement dynamics for dipolar interactions in 2D. Quantum Fisher information (left) and squeezing param- eter (right) as a function of Jr = J2/J1 for N = 49 (top) and system size N (bottom).

FIG. 5. Entanglement dynamics for the antisymmetric case. All-to-all interactions: (a) squeezing parameter and (b) quan- tum Fisher information versus system size N. Dipolar interac- tions in 2D: (c) quantum Fisher information and (d) squeezing parameter versus N.
